paper_id: Goncalves et al. - 2011 - Using an ECG reference ontology for semantic interoperability of ECG data

evidence_quotes.quote: In this paper we test the hypothesis that a domain reference ontology of the electrocardiogram (ECG) can be employed in an effective manner to achieve semantic integration between ECG data standards.

We have conducted an integration experiment which provides evidence that the ECG Ontology can be effectively used to support the design of interoperable versions of the ECG standards AHA/MIT-BIH, SCP-ECG and HL7 aECG.
then it could be used to support the design of interoperable versions 1 of ECG data formats like AHA/MIT-BIH, SCP-ECG and HL7 aECG.
A domain reference ontology can be used to support the design of interoperable versions of (otherwise) heterogeneous data formats.

goals.justification: The ECG Ontology is actively used as an instrument to achieve semantic integration/interoperability between ECG data standards and to support the design of interoperable versions of those standards. Signal pattern 'used to enable interoperability' applies, and the integration experiment was actually performed (not merely proposed).

evidence_quotes.quote: For reference ontology [10], we mean an ontology resulting from an application-independent representation of a given domain. In this case, the representation of what the ECG is , on both sides of the patient and of the physician.
As a reference model whose primary goal is adherence to reality, an ontology can serve as an anchor to both conceptual and terminological negotiation processes.

a shareable anchor is required for effective integration to be achieved. We have demonstrated in this article that a domain reference ontology (thus, an axiomatized conceptual model grounded in reality) can be used as a cost-effective means to foster semantic interoperability of heterogeneous conceptual models.

goals.justification: The ontology is explicitly framed as a representation of what the ECG is (domain conceptualization) and as a shareable anchor for conceptual/terminological negotiation. Signal patterns 'meaning negotiation' and 'conceptualize' apply directly to the ECG domain.

evidence_quotes.quote: Eventhough the excavated conceptual models we convey here are not meant to be final representations of those standards, to have them expressed in an explicit manner puts forth concrete media for discussion and elaboration in the community of Biomedical Informatics.

uncertain_goals.reason: This quote refers to the excavated conceptual models of the standards rather than the ECG Ontology itself, and the framing as 'media for discussion' is too vague to clearly establish a documentation purpose for the focal model.
